data.title: A Slightly Warmer Beer To The King
data.description:
Investment Thesis

All eyes are on King Charles' state visit to the United States, his first as a sovereign. News headlines have highlighted the contrast between the amicable reception at the White House and the rising tensions between President Trump and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer. Back home, Ed Miliband, the Secretary of Energy and Net Zero in His Majesty's Government, spurred fury and ridicule when the ministry rolled out a tool that "optimizes" fridge temperature levels to help pubs (bars) and other hospitality businesses reduce electricity consumption amid rising energy prices. This came after the country became the latest to declare a state of energy emergency in the aftermath of the Strait of Hormuz disruptions.

Investors are grappling with more serious issues than a slightly warmer beer in the UK. The fluidity of the Iran situation has created noise that I believe has distracted investors from more important secular trends. For example, the flight to safety has suppressed global equities and boosted the US dollar, breaking two secular trends that remain very much in play. The high equity valuations in the US and the ballooning public debt have created a secular rotation trend away from US equity into global stocks.

This trend will only accelerate. Emerging economies are set to benefit from the democratization of know-how through the advent of Large Language Models "LLMs", which I expect will create seismic cultural and economic changes in developing countries that far exceed those happening in developed countries, including the US, where the tech was invented in the first place. The irony is that Sam Altman's deviation from OpenAI's philanthropic origins is being examined in court, while the company's tech could become the single most powerful tool for narrowing the global wealth divide—a root cause of social injustice and various types of abuses.

Investors can capture these trends through the many global equity ETFs on the market. My preference is for ETFs tracking the FTSE Emerging Markets All Cap China Inclusion Index, such as the Vanguard Emerging Markets Stock Index Fund ETF (VWO). Here's why. First, the ETF is unhedged, offering exposure to a potentially weaker dollar by holding global equities in local currencies, possibly set to appreciate against the dollar as investors flee the US public debt mess and high equity valuations. Secondly, it includes a diverse set of developing market equities, capturing know-how democratization trends and their positive impact on emerging economies. Thirdly, it is cap-weighted, meaning that successful, large companies are rewarded with bigger representation in the index. Fourth, the fund has a low expense ratio of just 0.07%.

China Exposure Risk

China's political risk premium seems to be decreasing, providing an accommodative tailwind to the VWO ETF.

China and Taiwan represent roughly 56% of VWO's weight. This creates risk, given the potential hostilities between the two. China considers Taiwan part of its territory. However, this situation also creates opportunities. The general consensus in Europe is that while the US remains its strongest ally, the relationship needs insurance coverage in case it breaks, and China seems to be selling the policy. Earlier this month, the Chinese Commerce Minister said that China and the EU's dispute over EVs has reached a soft landing. Multiple European leaders have visited China in recent months, a manifestation of the closer relationship between the EU and China and alignment over key issues, such as support for the United Nations and international law.

Moody's rating raised the outlook for China, citing a resilient economy in the face of tariffs.

Beyond China and Taiwan, VWO includes multiple smaller economies that are poised for systematic cultural and economic change because of the advent of AI. These include economies that lack key infrastructure systems, with AI poised to fill in these structural gaps. These transformative benefits could spur growth much faster than the value added that the same technology brings to other established economies.

Last year, the South African equity market, which represents between 4% to 5% of VWO, was the second fastest growing market in the world. Middle Eastern markets represent roughly 8% of the ETF, including Saudi Arabia (3.6%) and the United Arab Emirates (1.8%). These countries have been embarking on ambitious and transformative socioeconomic initiatives, particularly in Saudi Arabia, starting from the lifting of the women's driving ban in 2018, and the more recent Saudi Vision 2030.

India grew by 8.2% in the first half of FY 2025/2026, one of the fastest in the G20 group.

The dollar is in a Long-Term Decline

President Trump's push for lower interest rates is often pitched as a driver of economic growth. I don't think that's the main motive. Government finances are being stressed by the high interest rates. Servicing public debt is now the second-largest account in the government's budget after social services, as government borrowing reached $39 trillion last month.

There is no political consensus over how to solve the problem, and the world is gradually realizing that this could be a systemic issue. This fiscal issue is putting pressure on the US dollar, whose global dominance rests on one single factor: confidence. Without confidence in the US economy, the US dollar as a reserve and international trade currency starts to make less sense.

Note: Since the start of 2025, the US dollar index has lost 9.2% of its value.

US High Valuation

In my view, investors should not wait for the perfect geopolitical conditions to start placing bets on the return of the established secular trends that have been disrupted by the war. That seems to be the crowd wisdom in the US, with the NASDAQ-100 (NDX), S&P 500 (SP500), and Dow Jones (DJI) at all-time highs, as investors bet on the continuation of an AI-fueled growth.

Emerging markets haven't yet matched the US markets, a manifestation of the heightened, but more importantly, temporary geopolitical risks that tend to impact emerging economies more. For those wishing to start betting on the end of the Iran hostilities and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, a better alternative would be emerging markets.

The VWO holdings now have a weighted average P/E ratio of 15.8x, much lower than the NDX (33.4), SP500 (25.4x), and the DJI (23.6x).

Final Thoughts and Risks

Most countries impacted or involved in the Iran conflict have strong incentives to end the hostilities fast for good reasons, including the midterm elections in the US, an exhausted Iranian military, and thirsty pub-goers asking for a cold beer. It is not too early to start thinking of the day after.

At the start of this year, VWO was outperforming the three major US market indexes, and up until the war, the gap was widening. Why? 1) Global equities were rising, benefiting from a rotational trade that saw investors fleeing expensive US equities and technology concentration into less expensive, less concentrated global equities. 2) The US dollar was weakening, as investors realized that the US debt issue might be more structural than once thought.

Emerging markets might offer a favorable risk-adjusted return as Trump continues his efforts to reshape the US's position in the world.

data.title: Best-Of-Breed Infrastructure Dividend Growth Machines Yielding Up To 7.6%
data.description:
Infrastructure (UTF) is one of my favorite sectors to look for dividend growth opportunities because it combines all of the major ingredients that I look for when investing. First, many infrastructure businesses have durable and defensive business models due to serving vital functions for the economy and being highly contracted and/or regulated. For example, utilities (XLU) companies like NextEra Energy (NEE) are highly regulated and/or contracted with their assets for lengthy terms, often with investment-grade counterparties, and therefore able to generate resilient cash flows regardless of the economic environment.

Many infrastructure companies are also able to command strong investment-grade credit ratings, given how durable and defensive their cash flow profile is, which enables them to carry a reasonable amount of leverage while still having a strong investment-grade credit rating. Since their underlying cash flows are often bond-like in nature, they have durable defensive business models and strong balance sheets. This then enables them to be attractive dividend payers and consistent dividend growers, because the strong cash flow and financial profiles enable them to consistently return a set amount to shareholders.

As long as they are prudently managed, they are often able to increase those payouts over time at a rate that meets or beats inflation, since many of them have inflation protections and/or inflation escalators on their cash flows and can invest in attractive growth opportunities to grow cash flows at least at a rate that keeps up with inflation and population growth. We can see this in many infrastructure companies, such as Enbridge (ENB), which has grown its dividend consistently year after year for over a quarter century, as well as names like Clearway Energy (CWEN, CWEN.A) Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP, BEPC) and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP, BIPC), all of which have impressive dividend growth track records.

It is simply a matter of waiting until one of these well-run, durable, and defensive businesses with strong balance sheets and impressive dividend growth goes on sale to the point where they offer an attractive combination of yield and growth. With this in view, today I'm going to discuss two dividend growth machines that are actually at the fund level, rather than individual stocks in the infrastructure space, that I think are some of my favorites to invest in for an attractive combination of yield, growth, and ultimately total returns over time.

The Midstream Transformation Story

The first opportunity I'm going to discuss is the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP). At first glance, AMLP's long-term chart looks unremarkable, if not bad, as the ETF's price has declined by almost one-third since its inception in 2010.

However, there are some important nuances to keep in mind, as the story has been much different over the past five years for the fund, as it has shot up 58.33% over that time. Namely, the entire state of the midstream MLP sector has changed dramatically over time.

Early on, MLPs had a much more aggressive and unsustainable model, where they would issue debt consistently to fuel aggressive growth spending and then would return virtually all their cash flow to investors. However, when energy prices crashed, concerns about overbuilding began to abound, not to mention the ESG investing movement emerged; all of these things weighed heavily on MLPs and prevented them from being able to raise sufficient capital, thereby leading them to slash their payouts, including some like Kinder Morgan (KMI), Energy Transfer (ET), and Plains All American (PAA, PAGP), in particular, in the wake of the COVID-19 outbreak, which caused energy prices to plummet.

However, since then, many of these companies have become much more conservative in how they structure their balance sheets, including paying down debt and deleveraging aggressively, emphasizing free cash flow generation, securing strong investment-grade credit ratings, and now putting not only their distributions but also their distribution growth profiles on a much firmer footing. This has been evidenced by the sharp rebound in the sector over the past five years, which has led to AMLP significantly outperforming the broader market over that period, despite it also being a very strong performance period for the S&P 500 as well.

AMLP's distribution growth has also been very strong recently, with it growing its payout for the past four years in a row, and for the past three years, its dividend CAGR has been a whopping 9.91%. It remains very attractive today, with its top holdings including the likes of PAA and ET, which, despite their checkered history, are some of the more attractively priced names that combine high current yields with solid distribution growth profiles and strong balance sheets, as well as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which is the gold standard for the sector. EPD has the highest credit rating at A- in the entire sector and an attractive combination of yield, growth, and unit repurchases.

Meanwhile, AMLP yields 7.54% on a trailing 12-month basis today, which, when combined with its strong dividend growth momentum, makes it a very powerful infrastructure investment that focuses on energy pipelines (MLPX) in particular. Additionally, I expect U.S.-based midstream, which is what AMLP focuses on, to benefit from increased energy export demand from countries in Europe and Asia in the wake of the Iran conflict and the Strait of Hormuz, as I recently detailed here. Therefore, I think AMLP combines an attractive combination of high yield, strong recent price performance, and dividend growth momentum, as well as potential macro tailwinds from increased export demand and AI data center demand for natural gas pipelines.

A One-Stop Infrastructure Income Shop

The other infrastructure name that I've been highly impressed with over time is the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG). Note that it is a CEF rather than an ETF, and therefore its price-to-NAV ratio can fluctuate over time. However, its price-to-NAV ratio has remained fairly stable over time, making it ETF-like in nature, since its 52-week low is just a 4.48% discount to its NAV. Its 52-week high is just a 1.71% premium to NAV. Today it trades at a 0.28% discount to NAV, which means that it is trading right around its NAV and is not particularly a good buy or expensive either.

Its current distribution yield is 5.66%, with distributions paid monthly. One of the things I like about the fund is that, for an actively managed CEF, its expense ratio before leverage expense of 0.88% is actually quite reasonable. In fact, it is roughly in line with AMLP's 0.85% expense ratio, despite AMLP being passively managed, so you're getting the active management here essentially for free relative to another infrastructure fund like AMLP, though it is higher than some other passively managed infrastructure funds out there. Regardless, when you combine that with a fairly modest leverage ratio of 18.5%, that is enough to juice the yield on the total returns of the fund without putting it in too much drawdown and margin call risk in the event of a market crash. I think the fund is well structured.

This is further evidenced by its impressive long-term distribution track record of sustaining or growing its distribution ever since the fund's inception back in 2004, while it also recently hiked its distribution over the past year by over 5%, from 19 cents per month to 20 cents per month.

The fund's long-term total return performance has also validated its approach, with it outperforming utility stocks over the long term.

Power, Pipelines, And The AI Boom

One of the biggest draws of UTG, in addition to its attractive monthly payouts, strong long-term dividend growth track record, and attractive long-term total return outperformance, is the fact that it gives you a one-stop shop to an actively managed and well-diversified portfolio of infrastructure. It has exposure to names spanning the utilities, general energy infrastructure, and power generation assets.

For example, one of its largest positions is Vistra Corporation (VST) and Constellation Energy Corporation (CEG), both of which are power producers that are expected to be big beneficiaries from AI-related energy demand. Meanwhile, they also hold a large position in CenterPoint Energy (CNP), which is a regulated utility that should benefit significantly from growing power demand. At the same time, they also have exposure to Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY) and other unique infrastructure assets that should provide valuable diversification for investors looking for well-rounded infrastructure exposure.

The Bottom Line For Yield-Hungry Investors

While neither of these funds is cheap when it comes to expense ratios, both are somewhat sensitive to energy price and interest rate volatility, and UTG in particular does not look undervalued at the moment. Both of these funds still offer a very attractive combination of current yield and dividend growth while also enjoying favorable macro tailwinds from things like:

  • The risk of elevated inflation.

  • Growing energy demand across the globe for U.S.-sourced energy, accelerated by the Strait of Hormuz crisis.

  • The AI-related power demand boom.

Thus, for investors who are looking for well-diversified and proven sources of attractive current yield and dividend growth over time and don't want to dig into the weeds of individual companies, I think that AMLP and UTG are very attractive options to consider. As a result, they are among some of our favorite alternatives to suggest to investors who want to avoid individual K-1-issuing stocks in our 7–8% yielding, total return outperforming, below-market-beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Dovish-Leaning Hold By The Bank of Canada
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US and Francesco Pesole, FX Strategist

Jobs and USMCA concerns keep BoC slightly dovish

The Bank of Canada left its policy rate at 2.25% as expected, but the overall tone of the accompanying statement leant somewhat dovish relative to market expectations. The BoC acknowledged the impact higher energy prices were having on headline inflation and inflation expectations, and Governor Tiff Macklem said that a hike might be needed if oil prices were to remain elevated for a long period. However, the Bank also emphasised that core inflation has behaved well and that the proportion of components of CPI that are above target has declined.

Meanwhile, the jobs market is still described as “soft” with weak hiring and job losses seen in sectors exposed to US tariffs. Growth projections also remain modest, with 1.2% GDP growth predicted for this year, 1.6% next, and 1.7% in 2028. Those 2027 and 2028 forecasts are a tenth of a percentage point below the consensus growth estimates.

While higher energy prices are a boon for Canada’s economy, given it is a major net producer of oil and gas, the ongoing uncertainty about trade policy continues to hold back sentiment. The coming evaluations of the USMCA trade deal between Canada, the US, and Mexico and the potential for the US to put more of a squeeze on trade partners prompted Governor Macklem to warn that if there are significant rule changes that harm Canada, then a rate cut may be required.

Our call is still for no hikes

As such, the BoC seems to be hedging its bets a little. For now, the Bank is prepared to “look through the war’s immediate impact on inflation”. Assuming oil prices come down, and US tariffs remain unchanged, “a policy rate close to current settings looks appropriate”. The upside risk stems from a prolonged period of elevated inflation costs that starts to feed through into broader price increases, while the downside risk is increased trade restrictions with the US.

In terms of our view, our base case is that the Middle East situation will gradually ease, we see a slow resumption of tanker flows through the Strait of Hormuz over the next couple of months, and energy prices do indeed start to come down. We also see the potential for a tough period of talks over the USMCA deal that will leave a cloud of uncertainty over the economy and weigh on the jobs market. In this situation, we continue to favour stable policy rates through to the end of the year.

Dovish BoC restrains CAD

USD/CAD briefly traded on the strong side after today’s BoC announcement, although market pricing didn’t shift meaningfully. Expectations remain for a 25bp rate hike at the October meeting, so more hawkish than our baseline call.

Today’s relatively dovish BoC endorses our end 2Q call for 1.37 in USD/CAD. We do see some downside risks to that forecast given resilience in equity markets and oil prices remaining more elevated than longer. But for now, we are assuming there will be a period of elevated uncertainty around USMCA renegotiations in June, leading to upward volatility in the pair.

We expect better CAD performance in 2H26, when a new USMCA deal could ultimately be agreed upon and the Federal Reserve should cut rates. We expect 1.34 by year-end in USD/CAD, with risks to the downside.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Southern Company: Positive Long-Term Clean Energy Pivot
data.description:
In an uncertain year for the stock markets, the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY) might be up by almost 5%, but safer defensives like utilities have performed better. The State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) is up by 8% YTD, for example. Interestingly, the subject of this article, Southern Company (SO), is the biggest holding in XLU after NextEra Energy (NEE).

SO, too, has seen over 8% price rise YTD, but that's not the only reason it's of interest. It's also a part nuclear energy producer, a sector with much promise and also the current focus of my investing group, Green Growth Giants. Here, I explore the significance of nuclear energy for the company, along with its financial health and stock metrics to assess its long-term prospects.

Nuclear energy's notable contribution...

While gas is the biggest energy source for Southern Company, contributing to over half the generation, nuclear energy had a notable 19% share in 2025 as well. It's just shy of energy generated with coal, at 20%, and that too because coal saw a 2-percentage-point YoY increase. As a result, nuclear went from the second-biggest energy source to the third-biggest last year.

...through two long-term plants

The company has two Georgia-based nuclear plants, Vogtle and Hatch, with generation capacity of 3.6 GW and 1.9 GW, respectively. Of these, Plant Vogtle, with four operational units, is the biggest clean energy generating plant in the US.

While Units 1 and 2 have been operational since the late 1980s, the real story pertains to Units 3 and 4. They were the first new nuclear reactors to operationalize in 30 years in the US, in 2023 and 2024, respectively. Powered by the Westinghouse AP1000 reactor, not only do they represent advanced safety features, but more broadly, they are also a reflection of nuclear energy's resurgence driven by the need for energy security and supported by global policies and rising electricity demand because of AI data centers.

Plant Hatch has an impressively long-term history, as its Units 1 and 2 have been operational since the late 1970s. They got the go-ahead for operating for another 20 years in 2022, and the company has now submitted an application to the Nuclear Regulatory Commission [NRC] to extend their operational life up to the 2050s.

Move towards clean energy through decades

A longer-term look at the company's energy mix reveals that nuclear energy's significance has grown over time and is just part of the overall move towards clean energy and away from polluting fuels.

While nuclear's share has risen by 5 percentage points to 19% since 2007, that for renewables has jumped by an impressive 14 percentage points to 15%. Natural gas has been the biggest gainer, though, now bringing in almost half the energy compared to just 16% back in 2007, while the share of coal has drastically reduced (see chart below).

Positive financial outlook

Coming to the company's financials, after a good 2025, which saw 11% revenue growth and a 6% increase in adjusted EPS, the company expects earnings growth to continue in the long term. Here's a look at both its short- and long term expectations.

Short-term earnings outlook

The company expects adjusted EPS to continue seeing a 5% increase in 2026. As a side note, it does need to be mentioned, though, that investors need to brace for disappointing earnings when the company releases results for Q1 2026 on May 1. Analysts' estimates on Seeking Alpha indicate that earnings could see a 1.4% YoY contraction.

But comfort can be derived from the fact that this is likely due to a base effect. In Q1 2025, the adjusted EPS had risen by 19% YoY. Also, growth is expected to resume in the remaining three quarters of the year, with full year adjusted EPS growth forecast to be 6.3%, which is higher than that indicated by the midpoint of the guidance range.

Medium-to-long-term outlook

Positive earnings are expected to continue going forward as well, with the three-year CAGR between 2025 and 2028 forecast at 4.5%, assuming that the earnings are at the midpoint of the guidance range in the end period. From 2028 to 2030, the company expects another 7-8% earnings increase (see slide below).

Stock metrics confirm long-term attractiveness

Based on the adjusted EPS guidance for 2026, the forward P/E is at 21x, which is higher than the stock's average five-year ratio of 20.1x, suggesting some correction ahead in the short term.

But by 2030, the forward P/E reduces quite a bit. To get to the adjusted EPS for the year, it's assumed here that in 2028, the figure would come at the midpoint of USD 5.35 and grow by 7.5%, also the average of the guidance range, by 2030. This yields an absolute adjusted EPS of USD 5.75 for the year and a forward P/E of 16.4x. This indicates some 22% upside to the stock.

This isn't a whole lot, but there are dividends to consider too. SO's forward dividend yield at 3.24% is slightly ahead of the utilities sector median at 3.14%. This isn't a bad yield, but it's not the highest either. It is made more attractive when two other factors are considered, though.

  • The dividends have been incredibly consistent, paid out regularly for the past 36 years. Further, the company has raised dividends for the past 24 years as well. This would be a significant positive at any time, but it's particularly so in these times of uncertainty. It's no coincidence that the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM) is up by a better-than-average 7.5% YTD.
  • Over a five-year period, the stock's dividend yield improves to 4.7%, and its contribution to returns becomes more visible. Along with share repurchases, the total return on the stock is 77% over the past five years, which is 30 percentage points higher than the price returns.

The high debt risk

The Southern Company story is essentially positive then, but one whose debt levels can't be ignored either. As of 2025, it had a high debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.2x. Seen from a historical perspective, though, this isn't too bad. In 2020, the ratio was even higher at 5.5x.

Also, the company's preferred measure, FFO-to-debt, has been improving, albeit slowly. In 2025, the ratio was at 15.4%, up from 14.8% in 2024. It now targets a 17% figure for 2029. The point here is that while the company could well hum along just fine even with high debt levels, as it has over the past five years, the number does make it more vulnerable if the situation were to turn adverse.

What next?

All in all, there's much to like about Southern Energy. The vast improvement in its energy mix over the past couple of decades goes in its favor. Notably, the company is at the helm of the nuclear energy resurgence in the US, having operationalized two new plants in the 2020s.

Its financial outlook is steady and healthy, with adjusted EPS expected to continue inching up over the years. Even as a short-term price correction is possible owing to an expected EPS pullback in its Q1 2026 results, especially as its forward P/E is also elevated right now, over the longer term, the stock looks good.

Not only are its market multiples more attractive, but the company's solid dividend profile also works in its favor. While the high debt levels are somewhat worrisome, Southern Company's target to improve debt metrics is of some comfort. I'm going with a Buy rating for SO, with at least a five-year investing period in mind.

Current month-end performance can be obtained at dodgeandcox.com or by calling 800-621-3979.

Market Commentary

Markets entered 2026 carrying momentum from late 2025, but escalating conflict in Iran quickly complicated the outlook and drove volatility higher. Surging oil prices stoked inflation concerns and weighed on investor sentiment. The U.S. economy showed signs of softening as payroll growth slowed and the unemployment rate hovered around 4.4%. Inflation remained above the Federal Reserve's 2% target, and the Fed held short-term rates steady at both of its first quarter meetings—a pause following three consecutive cuts in late 2025. Rising commodity prices have further dampened expectations for near-term rate cuts.

The S&P 500 Index fell 4.3%—its worst quarterly return in over three years. Higher oil prices and shifting rate expectations hit growth-oriented technology stocks particularly hard, including many leading AI companies. As investors shifted out of growth stocks, the valuation gap between value and growth stocks narrowed. However, market concentration remains high, as the ten largest S&P 500 constituents still represent approximately 40% of the Index's total market capitalization, and their sharp decline had a disproportionate negative impact on the S&P 500's returns.

Volatility also returned to bond markets. Early in the quarter, interest rates declined and credit spreads narrowed. However, the outbreak of the conflict reversed that trajectory. U.S. Treasury yields rose sharply over concerns that surging energy prices would reignite inflation, and credit spreads widened as investors reassessed risk.

Portfolio Strategy

We gradually adjusted portfolio positioning as market pricing shifted throughout the quarter. The Fund's asset allocation as of March 31 remains broadly consistent with the previous quarter: 47.7% in U.S. equities, 16.8% in non-U.S. equities, and 35.6% in fixed income securities plus cash.2

Within equities, AI sentiment continued to drive sharp divergences between perceived "winners" and "losers," creating significant valuation dislocations. A value-oriented approach is well-suited to this kind of environment. The recent volatility presented opportunities in select areas, including the Software industry, where we added to Microsoft and initiated a position in Roper Technologies, a leading software company providing critical systems of record for businesses. 3 We also added to the Fund's position in Booking Holdings, a dominant global travel platform encompassing Booking.com, Priceline, and Kayak. Booking's stock has been under pressure from AI-related concerns, but we believe its infrastructure would be difficult for competitors to replicate, and investors are underestimating the durability of its competitive advantage.

Within fixed income, we responded to market volatility by selectively adding to credit, while slightly reducing exposure to Agency 4 mortgage-backed securities that had performed well. New Credit holdings included: Amazon, AT&T, Salesforce, and the State of California. Despite these additions, the Fund's Credit weighting remains low relative to history, and we believe the Fund is well-positioned to take advantage of future opportunities within the credit markets. We continue to be conservatively positioned with respect to interest rate exposure, in recognition of the risk posed by inflation to both equities and fixed income.

Outlook

We remain optimistic about the Fund's long-term prospects. Its allocation across a broad range of sectors, geographies, and investment themes, combined with attractively valued equities and a diversified bond portfolio, positions the Fund well for a range of outcomes. Periods of uncertainty like this one have historically allowed us to uncover compelling long-term investment opportunities, and we are actively doing so today. While factors such as narrow market leadership and sector rotations may shape short-term performance, our focus remains on our three- to five-year investment horizon and long-term fundamentals. Thank you for your continued confidence in Dodge & Cox.

First Quarter

Equity sector allocation and security selection were the main drivers of the Fund's relative outperformance.

Equity Portfolio (vs. S&P 500)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Information Technology underweight position and stock selection, most notably Microsoft;
  • Materials overweight position and holdings, particularly Glencore;
  • Energy stock selection and overweight position, most notably Occidental Petroleum; and

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Financials overweight position, notably Fidelity National Information Services, HDFC Bank, and Fiserv; and
  • Position in Humana.

Fixed Income Portfolio (vs. Bloomberg U.S. Agg)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Below-benchmark duration position; and
  • Overweight to and selection within Agency MBS.

data.title: From The Factory Floor: 6 Robotics Stocks To Know
data.description:
By Zeno Mercer

Most robotics coverage in the media focuses on the headliners. The humanoid unveils the next big deployment announcement. While we do cover these newer and “hot” technologies in-depth, it’s important to remember that the majority of innovation and deployment in physical automation continues to happen on the factory floor.

Below, we highlight six companies in the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index (ROBO) that showcase a different side of the robotics story than you often hear in the news. Some have reported recently. Others are reporting soon. Taken together, they span the entire globe and are involved in almost every product you use. These are not companies you normally see trending on X.

FANUC Corporation (FANUY) (6954.T)

If you want to understand industrial robotics, start with FANUC. Founded in 1956 as part of Fujitsu and spun out as an independent company in 1972, FANUC is the company that automated the machines that make the machines. It commands the top global market share in CNC (computerized numerical control) systems and industrial robots, and its yellow machines are embedded in manufacturing facilities across automotive, aerospace, electronics, and more.

In August 2023, it became the first industrial robot manufacturer to ship a cumulative one million robots. Today, with over a million units running on factory floors across more than 100 countries, FANUC’s record FY2025 results provide a clear blueprint of where industrial automation capital is flowing.

In its fiscal year ending March 2026, FANUC posted record net sales of JPY 857 billion, up 8% year over year, with operating margin improving 150 basis points to 21.4%. For fiscal 2026, management guided 6% further sales growth and margin expansion to 23.3%.

The drivers: strong factory automation demand in China and the Americas and continued investment from customers navigating persistent labor shortages. FANUC’s tariff commentary is notable. After initial capital investment hesitancy, management said companies with sufficient liquidity are now investing again, partly because labor shortages in the U.S. show no sign of easing.

ABB (ABBNY) (ABB)

ABB is one of the foundational names in industrial automation. The Swiss-headquartered company spans robotics and discrete automation, electrification, process automation, and motion control. Its products are embedded in factories, power grids, data centers, ports, and electric vehicle infrastructure across 100 countries. If FANUC owns the CNC and robot controller layer, ABB owns much of the electrical infrastructure that powers the machines FANUC, and other ROBO constituents in the Manufacturing and Industrial Automation subsector controls.

That context makes its Q1 2026 results worth reading carefully. ABB posted orders of $11.3 billion against a consensus of $9.77 billion, beating it by more than $1.5 billion and up 32% year over year. Revenue grew 18%. Free cash flow hit a Q1 record of $1.3 billion. Management raised full-year guidance, targeting high single-digit to low double-digit revenue growth for 2026. The standout driver: electrification, where data center-related demand grew triple digits.

The same AI infrastructure buildout pulling through demand for Koh Young’s inspection systems and Han’s Laser’s processing equipment is simultaneously driving explosive demand for the electrical switchgear, power distribution, and automation systems that ABB makes. The factories building AI hardware need ABB products to run. The data centers running AI need ABB products to stay powered. Electrification is core to the supply and demand of robotics and AI.

Han’s Laser (002008.SZ)

Han’s Laser is China’s dominant laser processing equipment manufacturer and one of the world’s largest. The company’s machines cut, weld, mark, and process materials across electronics, automotive, new energy, and semiconductor manufacturing. It is the industrial laser equivalent of infrastructure.

Its 2025 annual report, released earlier this month, tells a similar story. Revenue hit $2.67 billion, up 27% year over year, a three-year high. Core operating profit, excluding non-recurring gains, surged 82%. Q4 2025 revenue was up 18.55% quarter over quarter, the fourth consecutive quarter of sequential improvement. The growth drivers tell you exactly where intelligent manufacturing capital is flowing: AI server infrastructure, new energy, and semiconductor domestic substitution across China. Looking ahead, management explicitly called out embodied intelligence as a 2026 opportunity.

Koh Young Technology (098460.KQ)

Most people have never heard of Koh Young. That is a mistake. The South Korean company is the global leader in 3D measurement-based inspection systems. In practice, that means Koh Young’s machines sit on electronics manufacturing lines around the world and verify, with sub-micron precision, that every solder joint, every component placement, and every semiconductor package is exactly where it should be. No inspection, no yield. No yield, no product.

Last week, Koh Young reported Q1 2026 results that deserve more attention than they got. Revenue of 72.7 billion won was up 42% year over year, a record first quarter. Operating profit jumped 209%. Net profit jumped 389%. The growth came from two simultaneous drivers: expanded sales of 3D semiconductor packaging inspection equipment to what the company described as a global No.1 AI data center optical communications module customer, and surging demand for its AI smart factory software across the same customer base. Both pillars grew at the same time. The company said that dynamic is exactly what they expect to continue.

IPG Photonics (IPGP)

IPG Photonics (IPGP) invented the modern fiber laser. The Massachusetts-based company builds the high-power laser sources that sit inside cutting, welding, and materials processing systems across manufacturing globally. Where Han’s Laser makes the machine, IPG makes the engine inside the machine.

IPG does not report Q1 2026 until May 5, but its Q4 2025 results, released in February, made clear the recovery is real. Revenue of $274.5 million was up 17.2% year over year, beating consensus by a wide margin. The stock jumped over 35% on that print. Management guided Q1 2026 revenue of $235 to $265 million, acknowledging ongoing tariff uncertainty. The underlying demand driver is the same one running through this entire piece: manufacturers building out AI infrastructure, new energy capacity, and precision automation are buying more laser processing equipment. As a bonus, they are now seeing new demand from utilization in medical and even counter-drone technology that feels a bit like Star Wars.

Microchip Technology (MCHP)

Microchip Technology (MCHP) is the backbone of embedded control in industrial robotics. Its microcontrollers, FPGAs, and analog chips are inside motor drives, robotic arms, sensors, and automation controllers across the industrial stack. When a robot moves, there is often a Microchip part deciding how.

Microchip spent most of 2024 and the first half of 2025 working through one of the worst inventory corrections in the semiconductor industry, which appears to have escaped from the bottom. In Q3 fiscal 2026 (the quarter ended December 31, 2025), revenue hit $1.186 billion, up 15.6% year over year and well above guidance. Non-GAAP gross margins recovered from 52% at the trough to 60.5%. Management guided Q4 fiscal 2026 revenue of approximately $1.26 billion at the midpoint, up 29.8% year over year. CEO Steve Sanghi called it a broad-based recovery across end markets, with industrials leading.

Robotics Ecosystem Takeaways

These six companies touch different parts of the robotics and automation stack, CNC and robot controllers, electrical infrastructure and power systems, laser processing and materials manufacturing, precision inspection, embedded semiconductors, precise robot arms, and manipulation.

Capital is coming back into industrial automation. The AI infrastructure buildout is pulling through demand for intelligent manufacturing equipment, not just data center hardware. Labor economics are reinforcing the automation case in the Americas and across Asia. Electrification is the connective tissue binding it all together. And companies that held margins through the cycle are now positioned to expand them as volumes return.

The continued march of Robotics as a fundamental part of our modern world has been the thesis since the inception of the ROBO Index back in 2013. What is clarifying right now is the breadth of confirmation. Across the world, the same buildout is showing up in orders, revenue, margins, and guidance.

The factory floor is speaking, are you listening?

ROBO is the underlying index for the ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO), the L&G ROBO Global Robotics and Automation UCITS ETF (ROBO.LN), and the Global X ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO.AU).

data.title: Prosperity Bancshares Is Building A Texas Banking Powerhouse
data.description:
Regional bank holding company Prosperity Bancshares, Inc. (PB) reported its Q1 2026 financial results before the market opened earlier today. GAAP earnings of $1.16 per share on net income of $116.3 million fell short of expectations by $0.16 per share. However, adjusted earnings of $1.50 per share on adjusted net income of $149.9 million topped projections by $0.06. Most other details from the press release were positive, with improved net interest margin and a decline in nonperforming assets. The company also announced that its merger with Stellar Bancorp (STEL) is set to close on July 1.

That merger is just part of the expansion story for Prosperity Bancshares, a company that appears to fall into the category of a serial acquirer. The company completed two deals during the first two months of 2026 and announced plans for yet another. On January 2, PB closed a transaction to buy the holding company for American Bank, which operates 18 banking locations and two loan production offices in South and Central Texas. One month later, Prosperity finished off a deal to purchase San Antonio-based Southwest Bancshares, which controlled 11 branches in Central Texas. In between these actions, Prosperity announced its intent to buy Stellar Bancorp, which would create Texas’s second-largest bank when ranked by total deposits. The $2 billion deal would add 52 branches to PB’s footprint, with most of them located in Houston and the surrounding area.

It is often challenging to parse out the impact of any one transaction when there are multiple deals taking place around the same time. The recent quarterly results contain several non-recurring expenses that detract from the overall performance of the company. However, I believe I see enough in the Prosperity story that makes it look like a stock to Buy, as the company is creating a banking powerhouse in Texas that should be able to take advantage of the economic strengths of that state.

Company Overview

Based in Houston, Texas, Prosperity Bancshares is the holding company for Prosperity Bank, a community-focused regional bank that operates in Texas and Oklahoma. The company also controls American Bank and Texas Partners Bank, which both operate exclusively in the Lone Star State. All totaled, PB lists 312 banking locations, with the majority of them in major cities like Houston (62 locations), Dallas/Fort Worth (61 locations), and Austin (25 locations). Tulsa has the largest concentration of Oklahoma branches, with eight.

At the end of Q1 2026, Prosperity Bancshares controlled $43.6 billion in total assets, a 13.4% jump from the end of 2025 due primarily to the previously mentioned acquisitions. Total loans for the bank were valued at $25.3 billion. PB also reported a portfolio of investment securities worth $12.0 billion, 12.6% more than at the end of the previous quarter. It is worth noting that the goodwill on the books for the company is now reported to be $3.8 billion, 8.8% of Prosperity’s assets, and a number that will appear in a discussion of the company’s book value later in this analysis.

The mix of loans held for investment by Prosperity Bancshares is relatively well diversified, with residential mortgages taking up the largest slice of the total at 31.1%. Commercial real estate is just behind that weight at 28.2%. Construction loans are third in rank at 12.9%. This portfolio produces a yield just under 6%, which is below what would be expected from a bank of this size, but the company has some added wiggle room due to its low deposit costs.

Deposits held by the bank at the end of Q1 were valued at $32.6 billion. Non-interest-bearing accounts controlled 32.4% of that total. This helps the bank keep its cost of deposits well below the average of its peers that hold a similar amount of assets. The total cost of deposits at the end of the last quarter stood at just 1.32%. That is two basis points better than at the end of Q4 2025.

Prosperity Bancshares is well on its way to becoming a Dividend Champion as the company has increased its annual payout for 22 straight years. The current quarterly distribution of $0.60 was raised from $0.58 in Q4 2025. The current payout ratio of almost 40% is slightly above the median for the financial sector, but not too far out of line. The current dividend yield for the stock is 3.45%. It should be noted that Stellar Bancorp has a below-average payout ratio but a lower yield than PB at just 1.6%.

Analysis of Prosperity Bancshares

The $1.50 in adjusted EPS in Q1 was $0.04 better than the same number from the previous quarter and $0.13 ahead of Q1 2025. Last year, the company reported adjusted full-year earnings of $5.72. PB is expected to earn $6.29 in FY 2026 and $7.28 in FY 2027, which would be a 10.0% earnings expansion for this year and a 15.7% earnings growth rate the following year. After several years of stagnant profitability post-Covid, the company appears to be moving the needle in terms of building up its bottom line.

Nonetheless, investor confidence in PB has not been particularly strong for the last three years as its stock has significantly underperformed the overall market. A purchase of Prosperity Bancshares stock made five years ago would be worth 7% less today, without consideration of dividends. Understanding the true value of the stock becomes more challenging when there is such a significant amount of goodwill on the company’s books. From one perspective, PB is currently trading at a discount to its book value of $81.40 per share. However, when tangible assets are considered, the tangible book value of the stock is only $42.39. The current price-to-TBV ratio for PB shares is 1.65, not exactly bargain territory.

Prosperity Bancshares has been making an attempt to enhance shareholder value through a share repurchase program that takes advantage of the book value discount. The company bought back about 837,000 shares of its common stock in the first three months of 2026 at an average weighted price of $68.15 per share. This was a total of $57.1 million of capital allocated in addition to its dividend payments. On January 26 of this year, the company announced a stock buyback plan under which up to 5% of its outstanding common stock may be acquired over the next 12 months.

What really impresses me with Prosperity Bancshares are the underlying metrics that make its overall operations so strong. The company has been able to keep its efficiency ratio under 50% every year dating back to 2015, when non-recurring charges are left out of the equation. Adjusted return on average assets was 1.42% in Q1, the third straight quarter where ROA was above 1.40%. Adjusted return on average tangible common equity finished the first three months of this year at 13.65%, four basis points higher than Q4 2025. Net interest margin rose by 21 basis points from the end of last year to the end of Q1 2026, landing at 3.51%.

Over the last 10 years, total loans at the bank have grown by almost 9% on an annual basis, but loan quality still remains strong, even through the recent acquisitions. Allowance for credit losses was 1.61% in Q1 2026, two basis points lower than the previous quarter. The ratio for nonperforming assets dropped 13 basis points in Q1 to 0.33%. The company did see an increase in net charge-offs in the first three months of this year, due to a $34 million rise in charge-offs for its commercial and industrial loans. The annualized ratio for the bank for charge-offs in Q1 was 0.67%. For Q3 2025 and Q4 2025, that ratio was 0.12% and 0.11%, respectively.

The final factor for me in deciding to give PB a Buy rating is the strength of its service area and the opportunity for future growth. Texas tanked second with 52 of the nation’s best companies in 2026, according to Forbes Magazine. As Stellar Bancorp is added into Prosperity’s operations, the company will have greater access to Houston, the fifth largest metropolitan area in the U.S. As of February of this year, the city is also the number two exporter in the U.S. out of 327 ports. Major companies seem to be moving to Texas on a regular basis. Even Oklahoma is showing promise, ranking in the top 10 states for domestic migration in 2025.

Risks to Consider

The overall health of Prosperity Bancshares is connected to the economy in its home state of Texas. Significant macro headwinds that impact the state would also have a negative effect on Prosperity’s ability to grow its loan portfolio and deposit base. Job growth in Texas was flat last year but is expected to pick up in 2026. Issues surrounding trade, energy prices, and labor shortages are all playing a current role in the state economy. Higher oil prices caused by the current conflict in the Middle East could provide expansion opportunities in Q2 and Q3 of this year. Areas like Houston, Dallas, and Austin are all seeing business inflows because of their low taxes and low levels of regulation compared to other states.

The challenges of such a flurry of merger activity like what PB has experienced include the risk that one or more of the new additions will not yield the expected benefits. Efficiencies may never be fully realized. Synergies may be more challenging as some customers for the recently acquired banks may see this transition period as a time to shop around their business to other competitors in the area.

The Bottom Line

Considering all that was taking place in Q1, Prosperity Bancshares appeared to successfully deliver a solid quarter. The company's aim to deepen its coverage of its home state is clear, and expansion into Houston adds even more potential for the year ahead. The sudden spike in charge-offs in the bank's loan portfolio is certainly concerning, and the company did little to explain a cause in its press release. This will be something to watch in Q2.

I do not see Prosperity Bancshares as a deep bargain, but the story here is compelling enough to consider its stock a soft Buy. In so many cases, the geographic area where a regional bank operates is just as important as the internal operational strengths of the bank itself. PB seems to be doing the right things in the right markets to build continued success.

data.title: American Century Focused International Growth Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Tectonic shifts occurring in markets due to major U.S. policy changes. U.S. policy shifts have caused the start of significant underlying changes to the global economy and geopolitical order. In the first quarter, non-U.S. developed markets advanced. During the period, investors generally favored value-oriented and defensive, low volatility stocks versus growth-oriented names.

Tariff confusion weighing on business leaders and investors. Uncertainty has caused business leaders to delay decision-making. In the U.S., weaker sentiment and consumer patterns suggest a potential stagflationary environment. Europe showed signs of modest economic improvement, and Germany announced plans for increased military and infrastructure spending.

Selection among consumer discretionary stocks detracted. An underweight position relative to the benchmark in broadline retailer Alibaba Group Holding hurt returns after the stock rose on news of Alibaba's artificial intelligence work with Apple and DeepSeek. On Holding was also a drag as the athletic shoe and apparel company's stock pulled back ahead of its scheduled earnings report. Online travel company MakeMyTrip was another notable detractor.

Health care sector holdings were a source of weakness. Novo Nordisk was a notable detractor compared with the benchmark due to implications of a study on weight-loss results from the use of semaglutide, while potential future pricing discounts for its popular weight-loss drugs also concerned investors. Biotechnology industry holding CSL also notably weighed on performance. Terumo detracted as shares of the medical device company sold off ahead of its earnings report.

Utilities sector position contributed to relative performance. Spanish multinational electrical utility company Iberdrola was the driving force of outperformance in the sector compared with the benchmark as the company continued to execute well, completing an acquisition and announcing a dividend increase during the quarter.

Goal and Strategy

Long-term capital growth by investing primarily in large companies in developed countries outside the U.S.

Societe Generale. The France-based financial services firm reported better-than-expected net interest income, supported by strong revenue across multiple business segments and stable costs for the period.

NEC. This Japan-based company provides information technology and communication services and produces electronics. Investors responded favorably after it reported strong earnings, upgraded fiscal year guidance and reaffirmed its final dividend.

Rakuten Bank (RKTNF). Shares of this Japan-based bank and financial technology company rallied on expectations that higher Japanese interest rates could lead to increased net interest margins and accelerating earnings growth.

Key Detractors

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. Shares declined on uncertainty about potential semiconductor tariffs and trade restrictions. The company also announced a substantial investment in U.S. chip plants, raising concerns about costs and their effects on margins.

Novo Nordisk (NVO). Investors reacted negatively to weaker-than-expected weight-loss results in a semaglutide study and potential price discounts on the product when it is added to the Medicare negotiation list in 2027. Weekly Wegovy sales did not inflect in March, also leading investors to worry the product would miss expectations.

Alibaba Group Holding (BABA). The portfolio's underweight position detracted from relative returns as the stock rose on news of Alibaba's artificial intelligence work with Apple and DeepSeek.

Notable Trades

Societe Generale. We initiated a position in this France-based banking and financial services firm as we believe its net interest income is poised to inflect. The company's net interest margins have improved with an aggressive cost savings program and a repricing of its mortgage holdings.

Alibaba Group Holding. Alibaba operates the leading e-commerce platform in China. We believe the company is well positioned to benefit from its new artificial intelligence-enabled products, which feature complex analysis and efficient deep learning models.

NEXTDC (NXDCF). We exited our position in the Australia-based data center operator and redeployed capital into opportunities with better earnings acceleration profiles.

Techtronic Industries (TTNDY). This power equipment maker manufactures tool brands like Milwaukee. We exited the position to reduce our exposure to cyclical businesses and potential trade policy headwinds.

Top Holdings

Our process is based on individual security selection. The portfolio continues to invest in companies we believe are strong and improving and have improvement that is sustainable. We seek to identify opportunities for sustainable earnings growth inflection.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. The firm continues to benefit from long-term growing global semiconductor demand. The company's confidence in its capacity plans is backed up by customer demand related to long-term megatrends, in our view.

SAP. The enterprise application software company stands to benefit from strong momentum in its cloud computing business and the secular artificial intelligence theme, which management believes will be transformative for the company.

London Stock Exchange Group. The financial exchange has a strong balance sheet, has demonstrated improvement and is growing business in an area of financials we believe is more shielded from fluctuating interest rates than banks and other financial institutions.

Societe Generale. The France-based banking and financial services firm has been inexpensive relative to its peers, and we believe its net interest income is poised to inflect. The company's net interest margins have improved with an aggressive cost savings program and a repricing of its mortgage holdings.

Tencent Holdings. We think Tencent's revenue growth will accelerate due to the Chinese government's supportive actions, which should improve the overall economy and help the company's advertising revenue growth. China's regulatory environment has also relaxed recently, and its approval process for video games has resumed.

Air Liquide. This gas supply company stands to benefit from the energy transition secular growth theme. Higher infrastructure, nonresidential construction and capital investment bode well for construction-related names in materials and industrials, particularly those exposed to sustainability trends, including renewable energy.

RELX. The company provides information and analytics solutions for professional and business customers across industries. RELX has started a multiyear product reorientation in its risk and legal business, driving a small upgrade to organic growth.

AstraZeneca. The British-Swedish multinational pharmaceuticals manufacturer is, in our opinion, set to receive a boost from several catalysts, including commercialized new drugs. We expect new drugs for lung cancer and obesity to help top-line growth.

NEC. We expect margins to expand in NEC's information technology services business as it restructures its unprofitable international operations and gains operating efficiency from its digital platform infrastructure. Japan's digital transformation provides a tailwind to support multiple years of strong revenue growth.

Keyence. Keyence is Japan's leading domestic supplier of sensors, measuring equipment, vision systems and programmable logic controllers. We believe Keyence is well positioned to benefit from a broad range of manufacturing trends such as increased quality control, traceability and machine guidance.

data.title: Bank of Canada Neutral Hold (2.25%) - USD/CAD Rallies To 1.3
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The Bank of Canada just released its policy rate decision, maintaining rates unchanged for the fourth time since October 2025 and, quite frankly, not hinting at much change in its stance.

The statement had nothing particularly surprising, with the Bank noting that the outlook isn't much different from that indicated in the January Decision.

Some concerns about the quarterly MPC projections regarding the economic outlook maintain the bank's view of a not-so-strong Canadian economy, which takes some pricing out of rate hikes.

Nonetheless, the BoC assumed a $75 crude oil barrel, so if it stays closer to $100 for the next meeting, the Bank should turn more hawkish.

On the loonie, it yoyo'd quite aggressively throughout the ups and downs of the Middle Eastern war – With WTI crude bouncing back above $100 just today, the CAD is seeing a two-catalyst recipe for its daily performance; at least against other major currencies (with USD traders awaiting the FOMC).

Even if the war really settles, the Canadian dollar should not regain its prior lows, with increased oil revenues and orders, which would underpin the CAD for the next few months at least – the BoC mentioned this in relation to oil developments.

"While the war in Iran may alter its composition, overall GDP growth is little changed in the updated forecast: Since Canada is a large net exporter of oil, higher oil prices increase national income even as consumers are squeezed by higher gasoline prices."

Let's dive right into a two-timeframe USD/CAD analysis.

USD/CAD Daily and Intraday Technical Analysis

USD/CAD Daily Chart

USD/CAD has officially stalled its correction, now bouncing from its 1.3660 support zone.

With the BoC not showing many hawkish signs, the CAD is immediately losing some strength, and this should normally extend the price action back towards the 50-Day Moving Average (1.37330).

Above 1.3750, expect to see further rallies in the North American pair back towards 1.39.

USD/CAD 1H Chart and Trading Levels

The FX pair has officially broken its downward channel, and having passed above its 50- and 200-hour MAs, the rebound should see continuation.

Look for a break above the 1.3710, particularly if the FOMC adds fuel to the fire in the US dollar – it will be Jerome Powell's ultimate press conference, so don't expect anything too crazy there.

Levels to place on your USD/CAD charts:

Resistance Levels:

  • 1.3720 – 1.3750 Pivot 50-Day Moving Average (1.37330).
  • 1.38 mini-Resistance +/- 150 pips
  • 1.3850 - 1.3870 Momentum Pivot (Channel retest 1.3860)
  • 1.39 to 1.3925 Support turned resistance

Support Levels:

  • 1.3675 200-Hour MA
  • 1.3630 to 1.3660 Key Support
  • 1.3550 Main 2025 Support (Range Lows)
  • End-January Lows 1.34820

data.title: Driehaus Emerging Markets Small Cap Equity Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The first quarter of 2026 saw a resurgence in global market volatility, as conflict in the Middle East drove a sharp rise in energy prices. At the same time, questions around artificial intelligence (AI) disruption of the software industry continued to intensify, while early signs of stress emerged in segments such as private credit.

During the quarter, emerging markets (EM) outperformed the MSCI All-Country World Index by nearly 300 basis points. Within EM, small caps lagged the broader index by approximately 60 basis points.

Performance Review

The Driehaus Emerging Markets Small Cap Equity Strategy gained 6.38% in the first quarter of 2026, outperforming the MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index by 712 basis points, as the Index returned -0.74%.1 Stock selection was the primary driver of outperformance, with the largest contributions coming from Industrials and Technology, alongside positive results from Energy and Materials. From a country standpoint, Taiwan, India, and Argentina were the largest contributors, while China, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates were the largest detractors.

Strategy Positioning

On a sector basis, the most significant increase during the quarter was in the materials sector, where the strategy added exposure to companies benefiting from tightening supply in critical minerals and improving visibility for uranium demand. Energy exposure also increased, supported by additions in Latin American exploration and production companies where operational execution remained strong and the fundamental backdrop continued to improve.

These increases were funded primarily by reductions in financials and consumer discretionary. In financials, the strategy exited or reduced positions where deteriorating fundamentals, rising execution risk, or less favorable funding dynamics weakened conviction. In consumer discretionary, exposure was trimmed as near-term earnings momentum slowed in selected retail holdings against a more difficult consumer backdrop in several markets.

Industrials remained the strategy's largest active overweight at quarter-end, reflecting a continued focus on power infrastructure, precision manufacturing, and aerospace-related supply chain exposures. The portfolio also remained underweight technology overall, though exposure was selectively increased in areas most directly tied to AI server demand, optical interconnects, and Low Earth Orbit satellites. The strategy continued to hold no exposure to communication services, which benefited relative performance during the quarter.

1 The performance data represents the strategy's composite of emerging markets small cap equity accounts managed by Driehaus Capital Management LLC (DCM) (the composite). These returns are estimated for the period as the underlying accounts' data is yet to be reconciled to the custodian bank. Net of fee returns reflect the payment of advisory fees and in some instances, other fees and expenses such as administrative and custodian fees while the gross of fee returns do not. Both are net of brokerage commissions charged to the accounts and reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings. The performance data shown above represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

Please see the notes section for other important information.

From a country standpoint, Korea saw the largest increase in exposure and moved from an underweight to a slight overweight during the quarter, reflecting new and increased positions in heavy industrial and power equipment companies benefiting from strong global demand for electrical infrastructure. India was reduced and became a deeper underweight, as softer growth expectations and broader risk-off sentiment across Indian equities weighed on near-term momentum. Exposure to China also increased, with China moving to a modest overweight, supported by selective additions in areas such as power grid equipment and other idiosyncratic opportunities. Brazil was reduced, though the strategy maintained its longstanding overweight to the market. The portfolio also continued to hold meaningful off-benchmark exposure to Argentina and Vietnam.

Sector Attribution

Industrials was the largest contributor to relative performance. The strategy's sizable overweight, combined with strong stock selection, drove the result as holdings tied to grid investment, electrical infrastructure, and industrial demand outperformed. Key contributors included a Korean heavy electrical equipment manufacturer, a Taiwanese precision components supplier, and an Indian power transmission company, all of which benefited from an accelerating capital spending cycle tied to electricity demand, grid modernization, and AI-related power needs.

Technology was the next largest contributor on a relative basis despite the strategy's underweight to the sector. Strong stock selection more than offset the allocation headwind, led by Taiwanese holdings tied to silicon photonics, server management semiconductors, and radio-frequency and waveguide components exposed to Low Earth Orbit satellite demand. These positions benefited from continued strength in AI infrastructure spending and improving visibility across select areas of the small cap technology supply chain.

Energy also contributed meaningfully to relative performance. Results were supported by both the strategy's overweight and favorable stock selection, led by an Argentine shale oil producer that benefited from strong operational execution, lower costs, and a more supportive upstream policy backdrop. A Brazilian offshore producer and a Greek refining company also contributed positively.

On the detractor side, consumer staples was the largest sector drag on relative performance, driven primarily by stock selection. An Indian spirits company was the largest detractor within the sector, as shares reversed following earnings amid concerns around potential excise tax changes, management turnover, and a broader pullback in richly valued Indian consumer names. A South African health and beauty retailer also weighed on results.

Health care was another source of relative weakness. Indian hospital operators were the primary detractors, as macro headwinds and broader market weakness in India pressured valuations. Although the strategy's underweight to the sector provided some offset, it was not enough to overcome the negative selection effect.

From a country standpoint, Taiwan was the largest relative contributor, as the strategy's holdings substantially outperformed despite the portfolio remaining underweight the market. Performance was driven primarily by Taiwanese technology positions tied to AI infrastructure and satellite-related demand.

India was the second-largest contributor, as an underweight position combined with holdings that held up better than the benchmark. The strategy's power infrastructure and defense-related exposures in India were important contributors and more than offset weakness in health care and consumer names.

China was the largest country detractor on a relative basis. Although the strategy carried only a modest overweight, holdings underperformed due to negative stock selection. A LiDAR sensor manufacturer was a notable detractor after guiding to weaker near-term revenue and a less favorable product mix, which pressured margin expectations despite healthy unit growth. A Chinese polysilicon producer also weighed on results as prices declined during the quarter amid ongoing overcapacity and regulatory uncertainty.

Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates were also sources of underperformance. In Saudi Arabia, the strategy's underweight was a headwind in a market that held up relatively well, and weak stock selection added to the drag. In the United Arab Emirates, an overweight position combined with disappointing stock selection across infrastructure and aviation-related holdings to weigh on relative performance, with a UAE-listed low-cost carrier among the primary detractors.

Market Outlook

We remain constructive on EM small caps, noting broadening of bottom-up earnings growth across several sectors and geographies, alongside a favorable macro backdrop. A weakening US dollar and positive earnings revisions continue to underpin the asset class.

Within EM equities, the small cap segment remains highly dispersed, creating a compelling opportunity set for active stock selection. Lower levels of sell side analyst coverage and institutional ownership contribute to persistent inefficiencies, which we believe can be exploited through active management.

More broadly, EM small caps remain under-researched and under-owned. This combination of dispersion and inefficiency is increasingly evident in a volatile and rapidly evolving global environment.

Until next month,

Chad Cleaver, Lead Portfolio Manager

This ratio will identify if a manager has beaten the benchmark by a lot in a few months or a little every month. The higher the IR the more consistent a manager is and consistency is an ideal trait. R-Squared is a statistical measure that represents the percentage of a fund or security's movements that can be explained by movements in a benchmark index. For fixed-income securities, the benchmark is the T-bill. For equities, the benchmark is the S&P 500. Standard Deviation is a measure of the average deviations of a return series from its mean; often used as a measure of portfolio volatility. A large standard deviation implies that there have been large swings or volatility in the manager's return series. Tracking Error is a divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark. This is often in the context of a hedge or mutual fund that did not work as effectively as intended, creating an unexpected profit or loss instead.
